original_abstract:
Non-targeted liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) serves as a cornerstone for metabolomics, facilitating the identification and characterization of small molecules within complex environmental matrices. In this context, data-dependent acquisition (DDA) is the predominant strategy for generating product ion spectra; however, its efficacy is highly sensitive to instrumental parameterization. This study systematically evaluates the impact of ten fundamental DDA settings on the Q Exactive Orbitrap platform using natural organic matter samples from marine, riverine, and terrestrial environments. 

By integrating classical and feature-based molecular networking (CMN and FBMN) via MZmine and GNPS, we assessed performance across 35 methodological variations. Performance metrics included network node counts, library annotation rates, multivariate clustering in principal coordinate analysis (PCoA), and MS/MS duty cycle efficiency. Our findings identify automatic gain control (AGC) target, mass resolving power, microscans, and dynamic exclusion as the primary determinants of data quality and coverage. Conversely, parameters such as collision energy, TopN, and isolation width demonstrated moderate influence, while apex trigger, monoisotopic selection, and isotopic exclusion had minimal effects on the overall results. These benchmarks provide essential guidance for optimizing non-targeted workflows on Orbitrap platforms, offering a framework to enhance chemical discovery in environmental metabolomics and beyond.
humanized_abstract:
Data-dependent non-targeted liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) is the workhorse for metabolomics analysis to detect small molecules in environmental samples. Data-dependent acquisition (DDA) is the most employed technique to generate product ion spectra. However, the optimal DDA parameters that maximize the quality and quantity of the detected features have not been yet clearly established. In this study, the influence of ten critical DDA parameters on Q Exactive Orbitrap performance was investigated using natural organic matter (NOM) samples from three distinct origins including marine, riverine and terrestrial environments.

Here, we investigated the performance of classical and feature-based molecular networking (CMN and FBMN) when combining data analyzed using MZmine 2 and GNPS across 35 methodological variations. The performance was evaluated based on number of nodes within the networks, the overall number of identified features that were annotated in a library, and on multi-variate clustering using principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). Additionally, we calculated the MS/MS duty cycle efficiency. Our results indicated that the parameters that had the largest influence on data quality and extent were automatic gain control (AGC) target value, mass resolving power, number of microscans, and dynamic exclusion. Parameters like collision energy, TopN, and isolation width had a medium impact on the data, while parameters like the apex trigger, monoisotopic selection and isotopic exclusion had little to no effect. With this study we have created a benchmark to optimize non-targeted shotgun tandem mass spectrometry on Orbitrap instruments and facilitate the process of finding new interesting chemicals with environmental metabolomics and other -omics researches.

The performance was evaluated based on number of nodes within the networks, the overall number of identified features that were annotated in a library, and on multi-variate clustering using principal coordinate analysis (PCoA). With this study we have created a benchmark to optimize non-targeted shotgun tandem mass spectrometry on Orbitrap instruments and facilitate the process of finding new interesting chemicals with environmental metabolomics and other -omics researches.
document.input:
Non-targeted liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC–MS/MS) serves as a cornerstone for metabolomics, facilitating the identification and characterization of small molecules within complex environmental matrices. In this context, data-dependent acquisition (DDA) is the predominant strategy for generating product ion spectra; however, its efficacy is highly sensitive to instrumental parameterization.
